coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of novel carbon dots based combustion catalysts and their effects on thermolysis and laser ignition of 1,1′-dihydroxy-5,5′-ditetrazolium dihydroxyammonium salt (TKX-50)
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zhen
Qin, Zhao
Zhang, Ming
Jiang, Yifan
Chen, Chao
Zhang, Jiankan
Zhao, Fengqi
coredata.dc:description: The carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by the oxidation etching method. On this basis, the composite materials of CDs and Cu (CDs/Cu) were synthesized by the hydrothermal method. The elemental composition, morphology and structure were analyzed by TEM, SEM, EDS, XRD and XPS. The effects of CDs and CDs/Cu for thermal decomposition of TKX-50 pyrolysis were studied by DSC-FTIR-MS and in-situ solid FTIR. The CDs and CDs/Cu reduced the low-temperature decomposition peak temperature of TKX-50 by 10.1 ℃ and 16.1 ℃ respectively (10 ℃·min−1), and the second decomposition peak almost disappeared. This is because CDs and CDs/Cu can effectively facilitate the thermal decomposition of TKX-50 into NH2OH and 1,1′-ditetrazole diol (BTO). The CDs can promote the direct decomposition of BTO into small molecule gases and stable polymers at the initial pyrolysis stage. And they inhibit the combination of BTO and NH3 into 5,5′-bistetrazole-1,1′-didiolate (ABTOX), which is more difficult to decompose. So, the HCN and N2/CO were increased obviously during pyrolysis while NH3 and H2O content were decreased. On the other hand, the CDs/Cu promotes the decomposition of NH2OH in the first reaction. It manifested as the increase of NH3 and H2O. At the same time, the CDs/Cu facilitates the transition of difficult to decompose ABTOX losing NH3 to BOX to accelerate thermal decomposition reaction, playing a synergistic catalytic effect. Thus, in-situ solid FTIR signal of ABTOX was significantly weakened and the total content of NH3 was raised, especially the significant expansion of NH3 in the second stage. Finally, the influence of the catalysts in ignition characteristics was contrasted and discussed. Under the lower-power density region, the CDs and CDs/Cu decreased the ignition delay time of TKX-50. The CDs/Cu caused the flame propagation velocity of TKX-50 faster. Therefore, the CDs and their composites, as another new lightweight carbon material, are expected to be applied in the catalytic combustion of energetic materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Composite
TKX-50
Pyrolysis
Catalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology, composition and structure of CDs and CDs/Cu

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) TEM and HRTEM images of CDs; (c) size distribution curve of CDs; (d) HRTEM images of CDs/Cu.
(a) XRD result of CDs/Cu; (b) EDS result of CDs/Cu.
DSC curves of TKX-50, TKX-50 + CDs, TKX-50 + Cu, TKX-50 + CDs + Cu and TKX-50 + CDs/Cu (10 ℃·min−1).
MS spectrograms of TKX-50 before and after mixed with CDs, CDs + Cu, and CDs/Cu: NH3(m/z = 17), H2O(m/z = 18), HCN(m/z = 27), NH2(m/z = 16), NO(m/z = 30), CO2/N2O(m/z = 44), and N2/CO(m/z = 28).
A comparison of the molar ratio of decomposition gaseous products from various samples.
FTIR spectrums of TKX-50, TKX-50 + CDs, TKX-50 + CDs + Cu, TKX-50 + CDs/Cu.
(a) Schematic diagram of laser ignition device, (b) Ignition delay time of three samples at different laser power densities.
Sequential images for the combustion initially process of TKX-50, TKX-50 + CDs, TKX-50 + CDs/Cu at the laser power density of 46 W·cm−2.
In-situ solid FTIR spectrums of TKX-50, TKX-50 + CDs, TKX-50 + CDs + Cu, TKX-50 + CDs/Cu.
Proposed pyrolysis pathway of TKX-50, TKX-50 + CDs and TKX-50 + CDs/Cu.
Nonisothermal reaction kinetic parameters of pure TKX-50 and TKX-50 mixed with CDs, Cu, CDs + Cu, CDs/Cu.
The ignition delay time of three samples at 4 different laser power densities.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of novel carbon dots based combustion catalysts and their effects on thermolysis and laser ignition of 1,1′-dihydroxy-5,5′-ditetrazolium dihydroxyammonium salt (TKX-50)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Science and Technology on Combustion and Explosion Laboratory, Xi’an Modern Chemistry Research Institute, 168 Zhangba Road, Xi’an 710065, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Science and Technology on Combustion and Explosion Laboratory, Xi’an Modern Chemistry Research Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Science and Technology on Combustion and Explosion Laboratory, Xi’an Modern Chemistry Research Institute, 168 Zhangba Road, Xi’an 710065, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by the oxidation etching method. On this basis, the composite materials of CDs and Cu (CDs/Cu) were synthesized by the hydrothermal method. The elemental composition, morphology and structure were analyzed by TEM, SEM, EDS, XRD and XPS. The effects of CDs and CDs/Cu for thermal decomposition of TKX-50 pyrolysis were studied by DSC-FTIR-MS and in-situ solid FTIR. The CDs and CDs/Cu reduced the low-temperature decomposition peak temperature of TKX-50 by 10.1 ℃ and 16.1 ℃ respectively (10 ℃·min−1), and the second decomposition peak almost disappeared. This is because CDs and CDs/Cu can effectively facilitate the thermal decomposition of TKX-50 into NH2OH and 1,1′-ditetrazole diol (BTO). The CDs can promote the direct decomposition of BTO into small molecule gases and stable polymers at the initial pyrolysis stage. And they inhibit the combination of BTO and NH3 into 5,5′-bistetrazole-1,1′-didiolate (ABTOX), which is more difficult to decompose. So, the HCN and N2/CO were increased obviously during pyrolysis while NH3 and H2O content were decreased. On the other hand, the CDs/Cu promotes the decomposition of NH2OH in the first reaction. It manifested as the increase of NH3 and H2O. At the same time, the CDs/Cu facilitates the transition of difficult to decompose ABTOX losing NH3 to BOX to accelerate thermal decomposition reaction, playing a synergistic catalytic effect. Thus, in-situ solid FTIR signal of ABTOX was significantly weakened and the total content of NH3 was raised, especially the significant expansion of NH3 in the second stage. Finally, the influence of the catalysts in ignition characteristics was contrasted and discussed. Under the lower-power density region, the CDs and CDs/Cu decreased the ignition delay time of TKX-50. The CDs/Cu caused the flame propagation velocity of TKX-50 faster. Therefore, the CDs and their composites, as another new lightweight carbon material, are expected to be applied in the catalytic combustion of energetic materials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The novel carbon dots (CDs) and their composites CDs/Cu were prepared and characterized, and their effects on the thermolysis and laser ignition characteristics to TKX-50 were studied and discussed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an energetic composite material with specific composite properties, the solid propellant is the power source of rocket, missile engine and other aerospace and weapon equipment systems. High energy propellant is the key base of high-performance missile weapon system development technology. Among them, 1,1′-dihydroxy-5,5′-ditetrazolium dihydroxyammonium salt (TKX-50) is a novel compound with high energy density. Compared with the traditional nitro compound energetic materials, TKX-50 gets rid of the restriction of nitro which will obviously reduce thermal and mechanical sensitivity. TKX-50 has a nitrogen-rich tetrazolium ring structure and it has no halogens. It is a new high energy density compound with excellent performance. The results show that TKX-50 has the same energy as hexanitro hexaazaisowurtzitane (CL-20), with a theoretical density of 1.918 g·cm−3, detonation velocity of 9679 m·s−1, detonation pressure of 42.4 GPa, standard enthalpy of formation of 446.6 kJ·mol−1, lower friction sensitivity and impact sensitivity than cyclotetramethylene tetranitramine (HMX). TKX-50 is a high-energy energetic compound with excellent comprehensive properties. The study of thermal decomposition of TKX-50 is of great significance for its production, transportation and application in explosive, propellant and so on [1,2] . As an important energetic component of insensitive propellants in the future, these characteristics of TKX-50 have a good application prospect in high-temperature polyethylene propellants [3–5] .

The propellant combustion is the key of propulsion technology Combustion catalyst is the vital component of solid propellant. With the continuous development of national defense and urgent need of engineering applications, it is particularly important to achieve high catalytic activity of combustion catalysts for adjusting the combustion performance of propellants, reducing the activation energy of thermal decomposition and enhancing the reaction rate and energy conversion efficiency [6].

In the current study of TKX-50, nano-metals and their oxides have shown excellent catalytic properties [7–10]. However, there is a general problem of agglomeration of nano-metal oxides. The studies have shown that using the high specific surface area of carbon materials to form carbon-based nanocomposites can effectually restrain the aggregation between nanoparticles and play a better catalytic performance. At present, more studies are focused on loading metals and oxides on carbon nanotubes (CNTs) or graphene oxide (GO) to increase dispersibility through weak interaction between metal particles and matrix carbon materials [11–16].

As a new type of carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have the characteristics of low cost, long life, excellent electron acceptor/donor properties and high electronic conductivity. So, the CDs possess broad application prospects in the field of catalysis [17,18]. This zero-nanometer material which is composed of ultra-fine, dispersed spherical carbon particles less than 10 nm in size, has played an important role in the field of photocatalysis [19,20] and electrocatalysis [21,22]. The CDs have a unique quantum size effect and are a new type of environmentally friendly material. The CDs and their composites have not been studied in the field of solid propellant combustion catalysis.

The introduction of CDs into the field of energetic materials provides a new feasible research direction for expanding the catalytic application of new carbon nanomaterials and their composites in solid propellants. The application of CDs and their composites in propellants is expected to change the problems of low yield, difficult process production and high cost of traditional carbon nanomaterials in solid propellant combustion catalysis. The different preparation methods of CDs can modify the surface structure, increase the active surface sites to improve the dispersion effect of nanoparticles and the performance control of propellant combustion catalyst. It also helps to further reveal the catalytic mechanism of carbon in solid propellants. Combined with the research direction of solid propellant combustion catalysts and the application advantages of the light carbon materials, CDs and the composite materials of CDs and Cu (CDs/Cu) were prepared and characterized in this paper. The catalytic pyrolysis and ignition characteristics effects of the CDs and CDs/Cu on TKX-50 were researched. The thermal cracking Ea of TKX-50 before and after mixing with CDs and CDs/Cu composites was calculated. The pyrolysis products of TKX-50 were characterized by differential scanning calorimetry, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and mass spectrometry (DSC-FTIR-MS). In addition, the condensed phase products in the thermal decomposition process were analyzed by in-situ FTIR. In the end, the ignition delay time and flame propagation velocity of TKX-50 before and after mixing with CDs and CDs/Cu were studied by CO2 continuous laser and high-speed photography. Based on the above experimental results, the possible mechanism of catalytic pyrolysis of TKX-50 by CDs and CDs/Cu was discussed, which laid a foundation for the research of CDs in solid propellant combustion catalysis, and promoted the application of CDs in propellant combustion catalysts.

All the chemicals are analytical grade. Medium temperature coal pitch powder, 30% hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), formic acid, acetyl acetone copper (Cu(acac)2), N-N′-dimethylformamide (DMF) and ethanol were purchased from Aladdin Inc. (EA). TKX-50 is from Xi’an Modern Chemistry Research Institute. Cu nanoparticles (Cu) were from Aladdin Inc. (60–100 nm).

The CDs were obtained by the reaction of medium-temperature coal pitch powder and mixed solution of formic acid and H2O2 without external heating by oxidation etching method. By controlling the reaction time and the blending ratio of formic acid to H2O2, small crystalline carbon was extracted from the coal bitumen powder [23].

In a typical experiment, 600 ml 80% formic acid solution and 60 ml 30% H2O2 solution were added to 4 g medium-temperature coal pitch powder. After stirring at room temperature for 24 h, the unreacted powder in the black mixed suspension was removed by suction filtration, and the brown solution containing CDs was obtained. The unreacted formic acid was removed by rotary evaporation at 70 ℃, and the solution was washed several times until it was neutral. Then, the CDs were obtained by drying at 70 ℃ ( Fig. 1).

The CDs/Cu combustion catalyst was synthesized by dissolving 200 mg CDs in 30 ml DMF, adding 10 ml water and 100 mg Cupric acetylacetone (Cu(acac)2) in turn. After stirring for 1 h, the brown solution was transferred to the inner lining of the reactor and kept at a constant temperature of 160 ℃ for 2 h. After cooling, the precipitation was collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm. Then, the precipitate was washed with ethanol and water, and dried in oven at 60 ℃ for 12 h to get brown powders ( Fig. 2). After the hydrothermal reaction, Cu2+ in the precursor was reduced to Cu0, resulting in composite CDs/Cu, the reaction equation was:
C + Cu2+ → CO2 + Cu0 [23,24]

The structure and composition of CDs/Cu were characterized by X-ray Diffraction (XRD). Using a Cu Kα source, the measurement angle range (2θ) was 5–90◦ with the scanning rate of 2 ℃·min−1. The morphology and size of CDs and CDs/Cu were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM Quanta600). The valence electrons and composition of the elements were described by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The structure of the complex was further characterized by fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and the catalytic activity of CDs or CDs/Cu on TKX-50 (the mass of CDs or CDs/Cu was 1/10 of TKX-50) was investigated by differential scanning calorimetry (DSC). Each sample was heated at 20, 15, 10, and 5 ℃·min−1, N2 flow rate is 30 ml·min−1, and sample dosage is 0.4 ± 0.2 mg. The pyrolysis products of TKX-50 with or without CDs and CDs/Cu were researched by differential scanning calorimetry - mass spectrometry (DSC-MS). Each sample was heated at a rate of 10 ℃·min−1. The ignition delay time and flame propagation velocity of TKX-50 before and after mixed with CDs and CDs/Cu were further studied by CO2 continuous laser (SLC 110) and high-speed camera. The radiation wavelength was 10.6 µm and maximum power density was 500 W·cm−2. The thermolysis kinetics of TKX-50 with or without CDs and CDs/Cu were researched by Kissinger and Ozawa methods (Eqs. (1) and (2)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, composition and structure of CDs and CDs/Cu
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology characteristics of CDs and CDs/Cu nanocomposites were analyzed by TEM and HRTEM ( Fig. 3). The TEM results in Fig. 3a and b clearly showed that the average diameter of CDs obtained by the oxygen etching method using formic acid and hydrogen peroxide as precursors was 3 nm ± 0.5 nm. The lattice spacing was about 0.241 nm, which corresponded to the crystal plane of graphitic carbon. It was the lattice spacing of the (111) crystallographic plane inside the two-dimensional graphite plane [23]. To observe the detailed structure of the ternary nanocomposite, the HRTERM analysis was employed. It could be seen from Fig. 3d that the composite material had two lattice stripes with different orientations, where 0.241 nm corresponded to CDs, while the lattice spacing of 0.208 nm corresponded to the (111) crystallographic plane of Cu [25]. That meant the small particles of CDs were combined with Cu particles.

In order to further observe the surface morphology of the composites, the SEM results were showed in Fig. 4. The results showed that the CDs/Cu composites had spheroidal structure with uniform size distribution. The surface of the sample was rough and irregularly convex, with an average particle size between 200 ∼ 300 nm. In addition, there was no obvious agglomeration between different particle sizes.

The XRD results of the CDs/Cu were showed in Fig. 5. The diffraction pattern of CDs/Cu sample had 4 obvious diffraction peaks at 43.3°, 50.4°, 74.1° and 89.9° corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), (311) crystal faces of Cu (JCPDS NO. 85–1236). In this case, the reducibility of CDs caused the Cu2+ particles to be reduced to the Cu0.

Since the CDs have no obvious XRD peak, in order to further determine the binding of CDs and Cu, the EDS test was performed on the CDs/Cu composite catalyst to analyze the corresponding element composition on the CDs/Cu composite catalyst (Fig. 5b). The result showed that Cu, C and O elements were spherically evenly distributed in the composite. This indicated that small particles of CDs and the Cu nanoparticles formed a large spherical structure with an overall size of 200 ∼ 300 nm. Among them, the carbon element comes from the CDs, and the oxygen element comes from the carboxyl group on the surface of the CDs. Combined with TEM, HRTEM, SEM, and XRD results, the CDs/Cu composites were obtained.

The valence state of Cu in the sample was further characterized by XPS. The displacement difference of binding energy was observed in the two samples ( Fig. 6). In the high-resolution XPS of Cu2+, there were two strong peaks at 933.8 and 953.7 eV, corresponding to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of Cu2+ respectively [26,27]. When the CDs/Cu complex was formed by adding the CDs, the displacement difference of binding energy appeared, and the corresponding strong peaks were at 952.45 and 932.2 eV, corresponding to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of Cu0 respectively [28,29]. The binding energy analysis of XPS spectra also confirmed the existence of Cu0 in CDs/Cu. This was due to the reducibility of the CDs [30,31], which reduced Cu2+ to Cu0 in the hydrothermal reaction, and finally formed CDs/Cu.

By adding the different catalyst with a mass ratio of 1/10 to TKX-50, the effect of CDs and CDs/Cu composites on the thermal decomposition catalytic performance of TKX-50 was studied by DSC experiment at a temperature rising rate of 10 ℃·min−1. The thermal decomposition kinetic parameters of TKX-50 before and after mixing CDs and CDs/Cu were obtained. In order to compare the catalytic effect of CDs/Cu composites, the thermal decomposition of TKX-50 by Cu nanoparticles and mechanical mixing of CDs and Cu nano-particle was also tested ( Fig. 7). As can be seen from Fig. 7, there were two continuous heat release stages in the decomposition of TKX-50. The low temperature heat release (220–250 ℃) and high temperature heat release (250 ∼ 270 ℃), and the peak temperatures were 240.1 ℃ and 262.8 ℃ respectively, in which low temperature heat release was the main heat release stage [32]. For convenience, the THDP stands for the high decomposition peak temperature, and the TLDP stands for the low decomposition peak temperature.

The addition of CDs made the decomposition temperature of TKX-50 wider (210–280 ℃), TLDP became 230.0 ℃ and THDP became 264.1 ℃. The addition of Cu nanoparticles increased the TLDP of TKX-50 from 240.1 ℃ to 244.1 ℃, but the THDP became less obvious even disappeared. The catalytic activity of CDs/Cu composite was higher than the single-component of CDs, Cu and their mechanical mixture. Not only was the TLDP of TKX-50 dropped by 16.1 ℃ (from 240.1 ℃ to 224.0 ℃), but also the high decomposition peak became less obvious. This has proven that the combination of the CDs and Cu not only united the advantages of the two, but also created additional synergies. The CDs/Cu had the best catalytic performance.

In order to obtain the apparent activation energy in the low temperature decomposition stage of the thermal decomposition process of TKX-50 under the action of different catalysts, DSC tests at the heating rate of 5, 10, 15 and 20 ℃·min−1 were conducted on the addition of catalyst TKX-50. According to the peak temperature of exothermic decomposition in the first stage of the DSC curves under different heating rates, the kinetic parameters of non-isothermal thermal decomposition reaction of TKX-50 were obtained by Kissinger method and Ozawa method, and the results are showed in Table 1. The results showed that with the addition of the catalyst, the overall TLDP corresponding sample sequence was TKX-50 + Cu ≥ TKX-50 > TKX-50 + CDs + Cu > TKX-50 + CDs > TKX-50 + CDs/Cu at different heating rates.

For comparing the effects of CDs, mechanical mixing CDs with Cu and CDs/Cu on the thermal decomposition products of TKX-50, DSC-MS technology was used to obtain the mass spectrum spectrograms ( Fig. 8a-d). The relative molar percentage of decomposition main gaseous products before and after adding the catalyst were calculated to compare the change of decomposition products. The main decomposition products of TKX-50 were N2 or CO, CO2 or N2O, H2O, NO, NH3, HCN and NH2 [7,32]. After the addition of the CDs, CDs + Cu and CDs/Cu, the types of gaseous products of TKX-50 did not change significantly, but the percentage of gaseous products did. The molar percentages of the main gaseous products for each sample were calculated and summarized ( Fig. 9). It showed that the CDs and CDs/Cu made N2/CO respectively increase from 42.5% to 53.3% and 44.0%. However, the gaseous production of CO2/N2O was relatively reduced. The relative molar percentage of CO2/N2O in pure TKX-50 was 18.2%, which was only lower than N2/CO in all gaseous products. Under the influence of CDs and CDs/Cu, CO2/N2O decreased to 11.4% and 15.8%. However, the changes of N2/CO and CO2/N2O were relatively insignificant when CDs + Cu were added to the mechanical mixture. These might be due to the fact that the addition of CDs made the carbon content richer, and was more inclined to generate carbon containing gas oxides. The oxidation of Cu itself would also consume part of the carbon containing gas oxides, so N2/CO and CO2/N2O content showed the opposite amplitude of change trend. In addition, the mixture of CDs and CDs/Cu had little effect on NH2 and NO, but the content of H2O, NH3 and HCN changed significantly. The CDs made HCN increased from 3.1% to 10.5%. Meanwhile the H2O and NH3 decreased from 13.8% to 9.2% and 7.6–3.2%. With the direct addition of Cu, the change trend was weakened, especially NH3 increased to 11.4%. Combined with the DSC results, these showed that the direct addition of Cu itself had no obvious effect on the decomposition of TKX-50, and even had an adverse effect. These three gas small molecules showed completely opposite change rules under the action of CDs/Cu, especially H2O (from 13.8% to 18.2), indicating that it produced more H2O during the thermal decomposition reaction of TKX-50. The addition of CDs/Cu promoted the transition of decomposition products from HCN to H2O and improved the hydride of TKX-50.

In the synchronous pyrolysis FTIR curve of TKX-50 ( Fig. 10), CO2 (2238 cm−1 and 670 cm−1), N2O (1271 cm−1 and 1305 cm−1), H2O (1560 cm−1, 3657 cm−1, 3756 cm−1), HCN (717 cm−1), NO (2205 cm−1), HCN (2238 cm−1), NH2 (3455 cm−1) and tetrazolium parts (975 cm−1 and 1181 cm−1) were verified [33,34]. With the addition of CDs, CDs + Cu and CDs/Cu, corresponding bands were also observed on the FTIR spectrums, but the corresponding temperatures were advanced. Therefore, the addition of the catalysts did not change the product type of thermal decomposition, but advanced the decomposition temperature of TKX-50, which was consistent with the results of MS and DSC.

As the beginning of the combustion process, the ignition leads to the combustion reaction of the energetic material. Therefore, the study of ignition characteristics and flame structure of TKX-50 and TKX-50/catalyst can further explore their combustion characteristics and evaluate the catalytic effect of catalysts. It is generally believed that the delay time is the interval between the laser emission and the light signal output of the photoelectric sensor to detect the ignited combustion flame [35–37]. The ignition delay time is related to the pyrolysis process, so the essence of it is closely related to the pyrolysis kinetics and pyrolysis mechanism on the combustion support or retardation of energetic materials. The ignition and combustion processes of TKX-50 and the addition of CDs and CDs/Cu were researched by a laser ignition appliance ( Fig. 11a). With the augmentation of the laser power density, the change in delay time is shown in Fig. 11b and Table 2.

With the increase of laser power density, the ignition delay time of TKX-50 before and after adding CDs and CDs/Cu all decreased at first and then remained unchanged. In the whole range of power density (10–60 W·cm−2), the CDs effectively reduced the delay time, and the effect of CDs/Cu was even greater than that of CDs. With the increase of power density, the difference of ignition delay time between the three gradually decreased to be close. This also proves that the addition of catalyst can effectively reduce the ignition delay time, and the composite material has a more significant effect. This may be related to the reduction of the initial decomposition temperature in DSC.

At a power density of 46 W·cm−2, the flame propagation images of TKX-50 and the addition of CDs and CDs/Cu are showed in Fig. 12. The CDs made the flame structure of TKX-50 more stable and brighter. The flame area also increased. These prove that the CDs are beneficial for TKX-50 to release and transfer heat during the spread of the flame. The addition of CDs/Cu significantly accelerated the growth rate of the flame. The flame showed bright color. The complete burning time was shortened evidently. The flame propagation speed was doubled and the flame area was further increased. The generally accepted mechanism of laser initiation indicates that the oxidation capacity of metal particles affects the sensitivity to laser [38]. This mechanism can be used to explain the further reduction in ignition time. The difference of ignition process indicates the change of chemical combustion mechanism. And it can be inferred from the flame propagation speed and flame area that CDs/Cu has a more significant advantage for the ignition combustion of TKX-50, which is probably due to the synergistic catalytic effect with the metal.

The decomposition of TKX-50 consists of two processes, primary decomposition around 240 °C and secondary decomposition around 270 °C, corresponding to two exothermic peaks in the DSC results. And the low temperature decomposition is the main decomposition process. The studies have shown [38–40] that in the first stage (primary decomposition), the salt of TKX-50 was dissociated into acid hydroxylamine (NH2OH) and BTO. Meanwhile, BTO could further catalyze the decomposition of TKX-50 and was a key step in the pyrolysis of TKX-50. The NH2OH has the lower stability, and it was first decomposed into N2, H2O, NH3, N2O and other substances. In addition, BTO is easy to combine with NH3 to form ABTOX which is difficult to decompose during the reaction [41,42]. But the formation of the ABTOX was reversible. In the second stage of decomposition, ABTOX continued to dissociate into NH3 and BTO, and then thermal decompose into smaller stable polymers and simple gaseous products. The results showed that the addition of CDs made the DSC curves move towards low temperature, and the peak temperature of low temperature decomposition decreased. This indicated that CDs promoted the decomposition of TKX-50, especially the first stage pyrolysis. Its catalytic effect might be due to the fact that CDs were obtained by stripping small crystallized carbon from coal pitch by peroxyformic acid and then assembling and growing it. The surface of CDs contained a large number of carboxyl groups and was acidic as a whole [23], so CDs could produce a large number of protons and promoted the initial stage of thermal decomposition. The NH3 is an alkaline gas that can be captured by carboxyl groups on the surface of mono-dispersed CDs. It can inhibit the formation of the intermediates ABTOX difficult decomposition, made decomposition toward favorable direction.

The DSC results showed that the addition of CDs reduced the intensity of the second decomposition peak of TKX-50, and there were almost no simple gas products in the second stage of MS, while the contents of NH2+/O• and HCN increased and the content of NH3 decreased which mainly occurred in the first decomposition stage. These suggest that CDs might effectively promote the decomposition of NH2OH and inhibit the formation of ABTOX. The MS results showed that the addition of Cu increased the content of NH3 in the first step reaction, which made it easier for BTO to bind NH3 to generate ABTOX, which was unfavorable for the thermal decomposition of TKX-50. Although the direct addition of Cu did not reduce the TLDP of TKX-50, it could make the high temperature decomposition peak weaken or even disappear. When the Cu combined with CDs to form the CDs/Cu, the decomposition was further advanced and the ratio of H2O, N2/CO was significantly increased. It can be seen that CDs/Cu promoted the decomposition of TKX-50 into BTO and NH2OH (NH2OH subsequently decomposed into H2O, N2 and so on), and the resulting BTO further catalyzed the decomposition of TKX-50 in the first step.

In order to further understand the catalytic effects of CDs and CD/Cu on TKX-50, in-situ solid FTIR tests were performed on TKX-50, TKX-50 + CD, TKX-50 + CDs + Cu and TKX-50 + CDs/Cu ( Fig. 13). The results showed that the decomposition temperature measured by in-situ solid FTIR tests were lower than that measured by DSC, which might be due to different experimental conditions and the high sensitivity of in-situ solid FTIR to chemical bond changes [43], but the overall change rules were consistent with the DSC results. For the pure TKX-50, 1585 cm−1, 1521 cm−1 (ν Tetrazole ring), 3221 cm−1 (ν-OH), 1410 cm−1 (δ-OH), 725 cm−1 (ν C-C), 1235−1 and 1165 cm−1 (ν C-N) were consistent with previous reports, indicating that TKX-50 began to decompose at around 230 ℃. The 1675 cm−1 corresponds to the absorption peak of ν NH4 in the formation of ABTOX by the combination of BTO and NH3 [44,45]. For TKX-50 + CDs and TKX-50 + CDs + Cu, the overall spectral bands were the same as that of pure TKX-50, and corresponding signals could be found in the wave numbers. It indicated that the addition of catalysts did not change the thermal decomposition process of TKX-50. However, the addition of catalyst changed the temperature range in which the band changes. The addition of CDs and the mixture of CDs and Cu nanoparticles made the decomposition temperature of TKX-50 occur near 220 ℃ in advance, while the decomposition temperature of CDs/Cu began near 210 ℃. The absorption peak strength of ν NH4 was weakened by the addition of CDs and its mixture with Cu nanoparticles, which meant that the formation of ABTOX was inhibited. The in-situ solid FTIR results of TKX-50 + CDs/Cu showed that the absorption peak of ν NH4 could hardly be detected during the whole decomposition process. This proved that the formation of ABTOX was further inhibited, which was consistent with the results of MS and DSC.

In addition, compared with the effect of CDs alone, the total content of NH3 in TKX-50 + CDs/Cu was increased. The obvious increase of the content mainly reflected in the second stage. According to the results of in-situ FTIR, ABTOX content significantly weakened or even disappeared with the increase of temperature (corresponding to the infrared signal of ν NH4). It can be seen that CDs/Cu can effectively facilitate the transition of difficult to decompose ABTOX losing NH3 to BTO to accelerate thermal decomposition reaction. Different from mechanical mixing, CDs/Cu are a composite combination between CDs and Cu. The CDs are monodispersed and homogeneous nanoparticles. When combined with Cu, CDs can effectively prevent the agglomeration of Cu nanoparticles. The contact surface between the two has a large number of defects, which is more conducive to electron transfer [46]. According to the quantum confine effect and surface energy-trapped theory, when the CDs surface states are excited by the functional groups, there are multiple electron transitions and transfers between the energy gaps [47–50]. Moreover, the defects themselves have certain energy and can stimulate electron migration [51,52]. This results in the rapid transfer of electrons to the surface and interface, which is conducive to the redox reaction. Therefore, CDs/Cu composite has the more effective synergistic catalytic effect on the direct decomposition of BTO into the final product. The pyrolysis path of TKX-50 is showed in Fig. 14 .

In summary, combined with the research direction of solid propellant combustion catalysts and the application advantages of CDs, the preparation of CDs and CDs/Cu materials using in solid propellant combustion catalysts and their application in the pyrolysis catalysis of TKX-50 were researched and analyzed. The CDs can reduce the TLDP of TKX-50. So, the TKX-50 can be pyrolyzed at a lower temperature which can improve the pyrolysis efficiency. The reaction mechanism is that protons provided by CDs can promote the initial stage of TKX-50 pyrolysis effectively. And the NH3 is an alkaline gas that can be captured by carboxyl groups on the surface of mono-dispersed CDs, which can effectively prevent the formation of ABTOX. In addition, the formation of the complex between CDs and Cu causes a large number of defects on the contact surface of the two. This accelerates the transfer and migration of electrons in the redox reaction and plays a synergistic effect in promoting the decomposition of NH2OH and BTO. It facilitates the transition of hard-decomposed ABTOX losing NH3 and becoming BTO to accelerate thermal decomposition reaction. The increase of BTO further accelerates the transformation of intermediate products into small molecule gases and stable polymers.

Finally, CDs and CDs/Cu also are found to improve the ignition performance of TKX-50 in the ignition test. The introduction of CDs and its composites into the field of solid propellant combustion catalysts has enriched the catalytic research on energetic materials by another new lightweight carbon material and its composites after CNTs, GO, carbon black (CB) and the other carbon materials. It is helpful to further increase the energy of propellants such as hydroxyl-terminated polyether (HTPE) propellant and improve their catalytic combustion performance.

1,1′ -Dihydroxy-5,5′-ditetrazolium dihydroxyammonium salt (TKX-50), as a new high energy density compound, is an important component of future insensitive propellants. It has a good application prospect in high-temperature polyethylene propellants. The thermal decomposition of TKX-50 has great influence on the combustion behavior of insensitive propellant. As a new type of carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) are the new type of carbon material after carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene oxide (GO), and have broad application prospects in the field of catalysis. But the CDs have not been deeply studied in the field of solid propellant. As far as we know, the effect of CDs and their complexes on the thermal decomposition of TKX-50 has not been reported. The agglomeration of nano-metals is an important factor affecting the catalytic performance. Carbon nanomaterials can effectively prevent the agglomeration of metal particles, so as to better play a catalytic role. The introduction of CDs into the field of energetic materials provides a new feasible research direction for expanding the catalytic application of new carbon nanomaterials and their composites in solid propellants. This will also help to further reveal the catalytic mechanism of carbon in solid propellants.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
